title: The Algerian War
summary: The war (1954–1962) through which Algeria won independence from France — fought over territory France legally treated as part of itself, against a Muslim majority excluded by a European settler minority. It brought down the French Fourth Republic, exposed the systematic use of torture by a democratic state, and became the twentieth century's defining case of decolonization as both catastrophe and reckoning.
hook: France did not call it a war. For years its official term was the 'events' in Algeria, or operations to keep order. The denial was not a slip. Northern Algeria was not treated in French law as an ordinary colony but as departments of France itself, which made surrendering it feel to many French officials and settlers like national dismemberment. So a struggle that killed hundreds of thousands of people was, on paper, a domestic policing matter. That fiction is the key to the whole story. It explains why France fought so hard, why it reached for torture, and why a democracy nearly destroyed itself defending an empire it refused to name.

beginner.theEvent: In the early hours of November 1, 1954, small groups of armed men carried out attacks across Algeria. They set off bombs and struck police posts and other targets. A new group claimed the attacks. It called itself the National Liberation Front, known by its French initials, the FLN. Its goal was simple and total. It wanted to end French rule and make Algeria independent. The war that began that night lasted almost eight years. It killed hundreds of thousands of people. The war lasted almost eight years. It helped bring down France's Fourth Republic in 1958 and ended in 1962 with Algerian independence.
beginner.context: Algeria was not treated as a normal colony. France had ruled it since 1830. Over time, France declared that Algeria was part of France itself, divided into departments like the rest of the country. Many Europeans moved there to live. These settlers were called the pieds-noirs. They owned much of the best land and ran most businesses. They held political power. The Muslim majority was far larger but much poorer. Most Muslims could not vote on equal terms. They had little say in how the land they lived on was governed. This deep gap between a rich settler minority and a poor, excluded majority set the stage for the war.
beginner.theRecord: The scale of the war is clear even when exact numbers are not. Hundreds of thousands of people died. The two governments give very different figures. Algeria's official count of its dead is around a million or more. French historians have usually given lower numbers, often a few hundred thousand. The true total is fiercely disputed and may never be settled. What is not disputed is the method. The FLN fought a guerrilla war. It also planted bombs in cities and attacked civilians. The French army used torture against suspects in a regular, organized way. This is established fact, not a claim by one side.
beginner.phases: The war moved through stages. At first the FLN was small and fought in the countryside. France sent more and more soldiers, eventually hundreds of thousands. In 1956 and 1957, the fighting moved into the capital. This was the Battle of Algiers. The FLN planted bombs in cafes and public places. The French army was given full power to crush the FLN in the city. It used arrests and torture on a wide scale. It broke the FLN network there, but the cost to France's name was enormous. After that, the war ground on in the countryside while the real battle shifted to politics in France itself.
beginner.mechanics: The FLN could not beat the French army in open battle. So it did not try. It used hit-and-run attacks, then melted back into the population. Its larger aim was political. It wanted to make Algeria impossible for France to hold and to win the world's attention. France answered with mass force. It forcibly moved millions of rural Algerians into regroupment settlements and controlled villages to cut the FLN off from food, shelter, and intelligence. Some Algerians fought on the French side. They were called the harkis. The FLN treated them as traitors. It also killed rival Algerians who would not follow it. Violence ran in many directions at once, not just between France and the FLN.
beginner.transformation: The war did not stay in Algeria. It broke French politics at home. In 1958, fear that the government might give up Algeria led settlers and army officers to revolt. The crisis brought down the whole political system, called the Fourth Republic. A wartime hero, Charles de Gaulle, returned to power. Many expected him to keep Algeria French. Instead he slowly moved toward letting Algerians decide their own future. Hardline settlers and officers felt betrayed. Some formed a secret group, the OAS, and turned to bombings and killings to stop independence. They failed. The war reshaped France even as France lost it.
beginner.causation: Why was this war so brutal? The answer lies in what Algeria was supposed to be. France did not see Algeria as a far-off colony it could let go. It saw Algeria as part of itself. Giving it up felt like cutting off a limb. The pieds-noirs had built their lives there, and many had no home they recognized outside Algeria. So France fought to keep what it called its own soil. The FLN, for its part, believed only total independence would end a century of being ruled and excluded. Neither side could accept the other's basic aim. That made compromise almost impossible and the fighting savage.
beginner.significance: Algeria became independent in 1962. The cost was staggering on every side. Most of the pieds-noirs fled to France within months, leaving the only home they had known. Many harkis were abandoned, and large numbers were killed after the French left. The war left deep scars in both countries that have not healed. It also became a warning. It showed that a democracy could carry out torture in the name of defending its empire. The writer Frantz Fanon, who supported the Algerian cause, drew on this war to argue about colonial violence. His ideas spread far beyond Algeria. The war remains the sharpest modern example of how painful and corrupting a colonial war can be.

general.theEvent: On the night of November 1, 1954, a barely known group launched coordinated attacks across Algeria and announced itself to the world. The National Liberation Front, the FLN, demanded full independence and rejected anything less. France's response set the tone for everything that followed: it refused, for years, even to call the conflict a war. The official language spoke of public order and 'events.' This was not mere euphemism. Algeria was legally part of France, organized into departments, not held as a colony. A settler population of around a million Europeans, the pieds-noirs, lived among a Muslim majority of roughly nine million. The first group held the land, the wealth, and the vote. The second held the numbers and almost none of the power.
general.context: France had ruled Algeria since 1830 and had spent decades insisting it was not a possession but an extension of the nation itself. That claim shaped the legal and political reality. Europeans held full citizenship. Most Muslims did not, or held it only on terms that required abandoning their legal status under Islamic law. Land had been transferred to settlers on a large scale across the nineteenth century. By the 1950s, an educated Muslim minority had emerged with no path to equality inside the French system. Earlier hopes for reform had repeatedly stalled. A bloody episode at Sétif in 1945, when protests were met with mass killing, hardened a generation. The FLN's founders concluded that the system could not be reformed, only overthrown.
general.theRecord: The human toll is enormous and sharply contested. Algeria's official position holds that around a million or more Algerians died. Many French historians have offered substantially lower figures, often several hundred thousand. The gap reflects both incomplete records and competing national memories, and it is unlikely to be fully resolved. What the record establishes beyond dispute is the character of the violence. The FLN waged guerrilla war in the countryside and a bombing campaign against civilians in the cities. It also killed Algerians who resisted its authority or backed France. On the other side, the French army's use of torture against detainees was systematic and authorized within the chain of command, not the work of a few rogue soldiers. Both of these facts are established, and an honest account must hold them together.
general.phases: The war's most studied phase is the Battle of Algiers in 1956 and 1957. The FLN had moved its campaign into the capital, planting bombs in cafes and other public spaces frequented by Europeans. In response, the French government handed General Jacques Massu's paratroopers near-total authority over the city. Using mass arrests, interrogation, and torture, the army dismantled the FLN's urban network within months. By the narrow measure of breaking that network, it succeeded. Yet the methods were widely reported and shattered France's claim to be defending civilization. The battle became the war's defining image of how a counterinsurgency can win the streets while losing the larger argument. After Algiers, the decisive struggle moved from the battlefield to politics in Paris.
general.mechanics: The FLN understood that it could not defeat the French army directly, so it did not try. Its strategy was to make Algeria ungovernable and to internationalize the conflict, drawing world opinion and the new postwar language of self-determination onto its side. The agency here was deliberate: a relatively small movement chose terror, attrition, and political pressure over conventional battle. France replied with overwhelming force. It deployed hundreds of thousands of troops and forcibly relocated millions of rural Algerians into controlled settlements to sever the fighters from the population that fed and hid them. Within Algerian society, the war was also a civil conflict. The FLN suppressed rival nationalist factions and punished the harkis, Algerians serving with French forces, as collaborators. Violence flowed along several axes at once.
general.transformation: The war's most dramatic effect fell on France itself. By 1958, settlers and elements of the army feared that Paris would negotiate Algeria away. Their revolt triggered a constitutional crisis that destroyed the Fourth Republic. Charles de Gaulle returned to power and founded the Fifth Republic, with a far stronger presidency that still governs France. Many who brought him back assumed he would hold Algeria. Instead he moved, cautiously and against fierce resistance, toward Algerian self-determination. The backlash was violent. Settler and military hardliners formed the Secret Army Organization, the OAS, which carried out bombings and assassinations and even attempted to kill de Gaulle. The institution that emerged strengthened from this crisis was the French state; the institution destroyed was the political order that had refused to confront the war's reality.
general.causation: Why was this particular decolonization so vicious? The decisive condition was Algeria's legal status. Because France defined Algeria as part of itself, surrendering it felt like national dismemberment rather than the loss of a distant territory. The presence of a million settlers who regarded Algeria as home, and who had nowhere obvious to return to, raised the stakes further. On the Algerian side, the FLN had concluded after decades of blocked reform that only complete independence would end colonial subordination. These were not negotiable positions but mutually exclusive definitions of what Algeria was. The historian's task is to distinguish this structural condition, an unbridgeable gap over sovereignty, from the specific triggers, such as Sétif and the failure of reform, that turned a long-simmering grievance into open war.
general.significance: Algerian independence arrived with the Évian Accords and a referendum in 1962. The aftermath was its own catastrophe. Nearly the entire pied-noir population, around a million people, fled to a France most had never lived in. Many harkis were left behind, and large numbers were killed in reprisals after the French withdrawal. Both societies carried the war forward as unfinished trauma, and French acknowledgment of the torture came only slowly across later decades. The conflict also entered political thought. Frantz Fanon, who worked in Algeria and joined its cause, drew on the war in The Wretched of the Earth, his analysis of colonial violence and liberation that influenced movements far beyond North Africa. The Algerian War stands as the starkest modern case of decolonization as both liberation and disaster, and of how a democracy can be corrupted by the empire it fights to keep.

advanced.theEvent: The interpretive problem at the center of the Algerian War is the relationship between French democracy and the practices it authorized. The war forces a confrontation that more distant colonial conflicts allow scholars to evade: this was a constitutional democracy, with a free press and parliamentary opposition, that institutionalized torture as a method of governance over people its own law defined as French. Raphaëlle Branche's research on the army's interrogation system established that torture was not incidental but systemic, integrated into intelligence practice and tolerated up the chain of command. The uncomfortable implication, developed across the scholarly literature, is that the abuses were not a betrayal of the republican order but an expression of how that order behaved when its claim to indivisible sovereignty over Algeria was challenged by the people it claimed to incorporate.
advanced.context: The legal fiction of Algérie française is the analytical key that distinguishes this case from other decolonizations. Because Algeria was constituted as departments of the metropole rather than as an overseas colony, the war could not be framed by Paris as the relinquishing of a possession. The juridical claim that Muslim Algerians were French nationals, while denying them the substance of equal citizenship, produced a contradiction the FLN exploited and France could not resolve. Benjamin Stora's work on the layered silences in French memory shows how this contradiction was buried rather than confronted: a war fought over whether Algerians were French was, after 1962, recoded as something that happened elsewhere. The settler population complicates any simple colonizer-colonized binary, since the pieds-noirs were themselves a heterogeneous group, many poor, many of non-French Mediterranean origin, whose investment in French Algeria was existential rather than merely instrumental.
advanced.theRecord: The casualty dispute is not a gap awaiting better data but a site where national narratives are constituted. The Algerian state's figure of over a million martyrs is foundational to the legitimacy of the postcolonial republic; lower French estimates, often in the range of several hundred thousand, attach to a different account of the war's meaning. The records that would adjudicate the question, comprehensive demographic data and complete military and administrative casualty lists from all parties, are incomplete, were never systematically compiled, and in some cases were deliberately not kept. The deaths from forced relocation, famine conditions in the camps, and internal FLN violence are especially hard to count. What the evidence supports is an order of magnitude and a structure of suffering; what it cannot support is the precision that either national figure asserts. Treating the number as settled, in either direction, mistakes a political claim for a demographic finding.
advanced.phases: The post-1962 phase exposes the limits of a triumphal independence narrative. The exodus of nearly the entire European population and the abandonment of the harkis, many of whom were killed in reprisals while French authorities restricted their evacuation, are not footnotes to the war but constitutive of its outcome. The harkis occupy the conflict's most analytically difficult position. Their service to France cannot be reduced to mere collaboration, since their motives ranged from local feud and economic necessity to genuine alignment; nor can the postwar killings be explained away as spontaneous. The independent Algerian state that emerged was shaped by the FLN's wartime suppression of rivals, prefiguring the single-party order and later the army's dominance. Extending the chronological frame past 1962 reveals that the war's violence was not only directed outward at France but inward, structuring the politics of the nation it founded.
advanced.mechanics: The question of whether the army's methods were effective, and at what cost, drives a live disagreement with high stakes. By a narrow operational measure, the Battle of Algiers worked: torture-derived intelligence dismantled the FLN's urban infrastructure. The military theorists who later codified French counterinsurgency doctrine drew precisely this lesson, and the doctrine traveled, influencing later campaigns elsewhere. Against this, critics argue that the operational victory was strategically self-defeating, that the very methods which cleared Algiers delegitimized the French cause domestically and internationally and accelerated the political defeat. The disagreement is not merely empirical but concerns what counts as success in a war whose decisive theater was political. To call the methods effective is to accept a definition of the war the army's defenders held and the eventual outcome refuted.
advanced.transformation: The war's constitutional legacy deserves more weight than narratives of decolonization usually grant it. The Fifth Republic, with its powerful executive, was born directly from the Algerian crisis of 1958, when the threat of an army coup returned de Gaulle to power on terms that let him rewrite the constitution. The institution that most shaped modern France thus emerged from a colonial war, and the strong presidency was in part an answer to the question of how a democracy could make and impose a decision its own settlers and soldiers violently rejected. De Gaulle's turn toward self-determination, and his suppression of the OAS and the generals' revolt of 1961, established civilian control over a military that had nearly seized the state. The war that ended French Algeria also produced the institutional architecture through which France has governed itself since.
advanced.causation: The deepest causal question is whether the war's brutality was contingent or structural. One reading locates it in specific decisions: the failure to reform after 1945, the handing of Algiers to the paratroopers, the FLN's choice of urban terror. Another holds that the integrationist legal fiction made escalation nearly inevitable, because a state that defines a territory as itself cannot negotiate its loss as it might bargain over a colony, and a movement facing a century of blocked reform cannot accept less than sovereignty. The two positions are not fully separable. The structural condition, irreconcilable definitions of Algeria, set the field on which contingent choices were made, and those choices, in turn, foreclosed alternatives. The strongest accounts resist both pure contingency, which underweights the integrationist trap, and pure structure, which lets specific actors off the hook for specific atrocities.
advanced.significance: Frantz Fanon's place in the war's afterlife illustrates how the conflict became a theoretical event as much as a historical one. The Wretched of the Earth, written in the war's final phase, generalized the Algerian case into an argument about colonial violence and the psychology of liberation that shaped anti-colonial and radical movements across three continents. The significance here is double-edged. The war supplied Fanon with the material for an influential theory of decolonization, yet that theory's generalization of the Algerian experience has been contested precisely for abstracting from the specific, internally violent, and politically ambiguous reality the war actually produced. The enduring question the war poses is not whether colonialism deserved to end, which it did, but what it does to a democracy to defend an empire, and what kind of state and memory such a war leaves behind on both sides of the sea.

foundation.theStory: The Algerian War is the historical problem of how a democracy fights a colonial war, and what that fight does to it. From 1954 to 1962, Algerians led by the FLN fought for independence from France. The difficulty that organizes everything is that France did not regard Algeria as a colony but as part of itself, governed as French departments, with a settler minority holding power over an excluded Muslim majority. That legal claim made the war unusually vicious and unusually corrosive, because France could not treat the conflict as the loss of a possession and reached for torture to hold what it called its own soil.
foundation.theDebate: Several debates run through the subject. The most charged concerns the death toll, where Algeria's figure of a million or more clashes with lower French estimates, and the dispute is as much about national memory as about counting. A second concerns whether the war's brutality was inevitable given Algeria's legal status, or the result of specific choices that might have gone otherwise. A third concerns the army's torture: established as systematic, it raises the question of whether it expressed or betrayed French democratic values.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Algerian War unlocks the study of decolonization, the limits of counterinsurgency, the politics of national memory, and the vulnerability of democracies to corruption in wartime. It is essential background for understanding modern France, including its strong presidency and its unresolved relationship with its colonial past, and for understanding contemporary Algeria. It also grounds the influential ideas about colonial violence associated with Frantz Fanon.
questions: France insisted for years that the conflict was not a war but a matter of public order, because Algeria was legally part of France. How did that legal definition shape the way France fought, and why does naming a conflict matter so much?

The army's torture in the Battle of Algiers helped break the FLN in the city, yet France still lost the war. What does that gap between winning a battle and losing a war reveal about what counts as success in a colonial conflict?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Scholarship on the war combines French and Algerian perspectives that long developed apart. Alistair Horne's A Savage War of Peace remains the standard narrative account in English. Benjamin Stora has been central to analyzing how France buried the memory of the war, including its long refusal to name it. Raphaëlle Branche's research established the systematic, authorized character of the army's torture, moving the question from allegation to documented practice. Fanon's The Wretched of the Earth functions both as a primary text of the war and as an interpretation of it, generalizing the Algerian case into a theory of colonial violence that later scholars have both drawn on and criticized for abstracting from the specifics.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is uneven. French military and administrative archives are extensive but were curated and, on torture, often deliberately silent; some material was withheld or destroyed. Algerian sources, FLN records, testimony, and oral memory, recover the movement's side but carry the imprint of the single-party state that emerged. The casualty figures cannot be reconciled because the demographic and military records that would settle them were never fully compiled. The evidence firmly supports the systematic nature of torture and the broad scale of death and displacement. It cannot deliver a precise death toll, a full accounting of harki killings, or a complete record of internal FLN violence.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument about the war must take a position on its central tension: whether the brutality flowed from Algeria's legal status or from avoidable choices. The most defensible position holds both together rather than choosing. The integrationist fiction created a structural trap, since a state that calls a territory part of itself cannot bargain its loss, and a movement facing a century of blocked reform will accept nothing short of sovereignty; that trap made escalation likely. But it did not dictate the specific decision to authorize torture in Algiers or the FLN's choice of civilian bombing, and those choices carry their own responsibility. A position that blames only structure absolves the actors; one that blames only choices ignores the field on which they acted. The argument must also refuse to let an operational success, the breaking of the FLN network, stand as a strategic one, since the war's decisive theater was political and France lost it.
questions: Raphaëlle Branche and others have shown that torture was systematic and authorized rather than the act of rogue soldiers. How should that finding shape the way we assign responsibility, and what does it imply about whether the abuses betrayed or expressed the French republican order?

The death toll is claimed at over a million by Algeria and at much lower figures by many French historians. Given that the records to settle it do not exist, how should a historian write about the number without endorsing either national narrative as established fact?

research.topic: The systematic and authorized use of torture by French forces
research.content: It is established, principally through Raphaëlle Branche's archival research and corroborating testimony and official documents, that the French army's use of torture during the Algerian War was systematic and integrated into its intelligence practice rather than the isolated misconduct of individuals. During the Battle of Algiers, torture-derived information was used to dismantle the FLN's urban network, and the practice was tolerated and in effect authorized within the chain of command. This finding moved the subject from contested allegation to documented institutional history. It is central to the entry's argument because it establishes that a democratic state with a free press and parliamentary opposition institutionalized torture in defense of territory it legally defined as its own. The finding does not depend on either side's wartime propaganda; it rests on the army's own records and the later testimony of participants.

research.topic: Memory, recognition, and the slow French reckoning with the war
research.content: Scholarship associated with Benjamin Stora and others has reframed the war as much through its afterlife in memory as through its events. France long refused to call the conflict a war, officially adopting the term only decades later, and the experiences of the harkis, the pieds-noirs, and Algerian victims were submerged in competing and largely separate national memories. Recent decades have seen partial official acknowledgments in France regarding specific cases of torture and disappearance, and a continuing, politically charged process of recognition between the two states. This work is still developing: archives continue to open unevenly, official positions shift, and the relationship between the French and Algerian accounts of the war remains unsettled. The emerging picture treats memory itself as a historical object, showing how a war fought over whether Algerians were French was, after 1962, recoded on both sides of the Mediterranean.

research.topic: The precise total death toll of the war
research.content: The precise number of people killed in the Algerian War cannot be established. What evidence would resolve it: comprehensive demographic surveys of the Algerian population before and after the conflict, combined with complete and reliable casualty records from the French military, the FLN, and civilian authorities, including deaths in relocation camps and from internal FLN violence and harki reprisals. Why unavailable: such records were never systematically compiled; French administrative and military records are incomplete and on sensitive matters were curated or destroyed; FLN casualty records were partial and shaped by the movement's later political needs; and deaths from displacement, famine conditions, and reprisal killings went largely uncounted. What can be responsibly inferred: that the dead numbered in the hundreds of thousands at minimum, that civilians bore a heavy share, and that the order of magnitude is not seriously in doubt even as the exact figure is. What must remain unknowable: a precise total. Algeria's official figure of around a million or more and the lower estimates common among French historians are best understood as competing national claims that the surviving evidence cannot adjudicate.

comparativeNarrative.name: The War of Liberation
comparativeNarrative.content: In Algeria, the conflict is the war of liberation, the founding event of the independent nation. It is remembered as the climax of more than a century of resistance to a settler colonialism that took the best land and shut the majority out of power. The dead, counted officially at over a million martyrs, anchor the legitimacy of the republic that followed. This tradition tends to present the movement as more unified than it really was. It has less room for the FLN's suppression of rivals and its violence against Algerians who would not follow it. What it captures, and what outside accounts often miss, is the experience of a people governed as subjects in their own land, who saw independence as a stolen sovereignty regained.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Lost Homeland and the Abandoned
comparativeNarrative.content: For the pieds-noirs, the European settlers, and for the harkis who fought alongside France, the war ended in exile or abandonment. Most of the settler population fled to France in 1962. They left the only home their families had known for generations, and arrived as outsiders in a country many had never seen. The harkis fared worse. Left behind at independence, many were killed in reprisals, and survivors in France have long struggled for recognition. This memory resists the simple story of colonizer and colonized. Many pieds-noirs were poor, and the harkis' choices ranged from conviction to coercion. What it preserves is the human cost borne by those on the losing side of independence, a cost that triumphal accounts on either side tend to pass over quickly.

comparativeNarrative.name: The War That Could Not Be Named
comparativeNarrative.content: In France, the war was for decades a silence as much as a memory. The state refused to call it a war. The torture was not officially admitted. The conflict that nearly destroyed the republic was recoded as something that had happened elsewhere. The establishment remembered 1958 mainly as the birth of the stronger Fifth Republic, not as a colonial crisis that returned de Gaulle to power. Only slowly, across later decades, did French scholars and eventually parts of the state confront the systematic torture and the war's true name. What this tradition reveals, when it finally speaks, is how a democracy shields itself from its own past, and how long the reckoning with a colonial war can be deferred even where the press is free.

comparativeSummary: The same eight years carry three incompatible meanings. For Algeria, the war is liberation, the founding of a nation whose martyrs legitimize the state, a story that has little room for the movement's own internal violence. For the pieds-noirs and the harkis, it is loss: exile for the settlers, abandonment and reprisal for the Algerians who served France, a human cost both national triumphs hurry past. For France, it was first a silence, a war it would not name and a torture it would not admit, confronted only slowly. Holding the three together shows that the war's deepest difficulty is not establishing what happened but reconciling who is allowed to count as its victim, and that no single national memory can do that work alone.

rabbitHole.label: Decolonization
rabbitHole.reason: The Algerian War is one of the defining episodes of twentieth-century decolonization, and the broad process entry supplies the wider pattern of European withdrawal into which this specific, exceptionally violent case fits.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: The war was the violent unmaking of a settler colonialism that had transferred land and power to a European minority; the colonialism entry provides the structures of extraction and exclusion that made Algeria's Muslim majority subjects in their own country.

rabbitHole.label: Frantz Fanon
rabbitHole.reason: Fanon worked in Algeria and joined its cause, and The Wretched of the Earth generalized this war into an influential theory of colonial violence and liberation; understanding the war's place in anti-colonial thought begins with him.

rabbitHole.label: The Wretched of the Earth
rabbitHole.reason: Fanon's central text was written in the war's final phase and drew directly on the Algerian case; it is the primary work through which the conflict entered global political thought.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: The FLN fused Algerian national identity with anti-colonial struggle, and the war turned on irreconcilable claims to sovereignty over the same territory, making it a sharp case of the nationalism entry's core problem.

rabbitHole.label: The Cold War
rabbitHole.reason: The war unfolded within the postwar order of self-determination and superpower competition that shaped how the FLN internationalized its cause and how France justified holding Algeria, the same global frame in which other decolonizations played out.

rabbitHole.label: The Vietnam War
rabbitHole.reason: France fought a parallel anti-colonial war in Indochina that it lost at Dien Bien Phu in 1954, just as the Algerian War began; both show a European democracy waging a colonial counterinsurgency it could not win, and the comparison sharpens what was specific to Algeria.

rabbitHole.label: The Mau Mau Uprising
rabbitHole.reason: The British counterinsurgency against the Mau Mau in Kenya is the closest parallel: another European democracy in another settler colony using mass detention and brutal methods against an anti-colonial revolt, offering a direct comparison to French conduct in Algeria.

reference.author: Alistair Horne
reference.title: A Savage War of Peace: Algeria 1954–1962
reference.annotation: The standard English-language narrative, supplying the entry's chronology and its account of the major episodes from the November 1954 attacks through the Battle of Algiers to Évian. Comprehensive and highly readable, it carries the course of events that later, more specialized work on torture and memory then deepens.

reference.author: Raphaëlle Branche
reference.title: La torture et l'armée pendant la guerre d'Algérie
reference.annotation: The definitive study establishing that the French army's torture was systematic and integrated into its intelligence practice rather than incidental. It underpins the entry's treatment of torture as documented institutional fact and is the work that moved the question from allegation to evidence.

reference.author: Benjamin Stora
reference.title: La gangrène et l'oubli: La mémoire de la guerre d'Algérie
reference.annotation: The foundational analysis of how France buried the memory of the war, including its long refusal to name it. It supplies the entry's account of silence and recognition and of the competing national memories on both sides of the Mediterranean.

reference.author: Frantz Fanon
reference.title: The Wretched of the Earth
reference.annotation: Written in the war's final phase by a participant in the Algerian cause, this is both a primary document of the conflict and the text through which it entered global political thought. The entry treats it as the source of the war's theoretical afterlife rather than re-narrating Fanon's biography.

reference.author: Martin Evans
reference.title: Algeria: France's Undeclared War
reference.annotation: A more recent scholarly synthesis that integrates Algerian and French perspectives and incorporates later research on violence and memory. Useful for readers who want a current single-volume account that updates Horne's narrative with subsequent scholarship.

reference.author: Matthew Connelly
reference.title: A Diplomatic Revolution: Algeria's Fight for Independence and the Origins of the Post-Cold War Era
reference.annotation: Analyzes how the FLN internationalized the conflict and turned world opinion and the postwar language of self-determination into a strategic weapon. Supplies the entry's account of the war's political and diplomatic theater beyond the battlefield.

commerce.title: A Savage War of Peace: Algeria 1954–1962
commerce.author: Alistair Horne
commerce.note: Best first book if you have never read anything on the war. Horne's narrative carries you through the whole conflict with clarity and momentum, and most readers should finish it before going deeper into the scholarship.

commerce.title: Algeria: France's Undeclared War
commerce.author: Martin Evans
commerce.note: Read this after the narrative when you want a more recent account that weighs French and Algerian perspectives together and draws on later research into torture and memory. Serious but accessible to a non-specialist.

commerce.title: A Diplomatic Revolution: Algeria's Fight for Independence and the Origins of the Post-Cold War Era
commerce.author: Matthew Connelly
commerce.note: For readers ready for an academic argument, this reframes the war as a diplomatic and international struggle. Best once you already know the basic chronology and want to understand how the FLN won abroad as well as at home.

commerce.title: The Wretched of the Earth
commerce.author: Frantz Fanon
commerce.note: Read this to understand how the war entered political thought. It is a primary text written from inside the Algerian cause, demanding but foundational for anyone tracing the ideas about colonial violence the war produced.

commerce.title: A History of Algeria
commerce.author: James McDougall
commerce.note: Turn to this when you want the war set inside the longer Algerian story rather than studied in isolation. McDougall covers the colonial century before 1954 and the decades after independence, so the war reads as one chapter in a deeper history.

popularCulture.title: The Battle of Algiers — Gillo Pontecorvo (1966)
popularCulture.description: Shot like a newsreel, this film follows the FLN's bombing campaign in the capital and the French army's crackdown. It shows torture and terror plainly on both sides. For decades militaries and rebels alike have studied it. Its power can make the messy, longer war feel as tight and clear as a single battle, which it was not.

popularCulture.title: Algérie française
popularCulture.description: This phrase, French Algeria, was the rallying cry of settlers and soldiers who wanted Algeria to stay part of France. It became shorthand for the whole idea that Algeria was not a colony but French soil. The slogan still echoes in French politics. It can make the settlers sound united, when in truth many were poor and feared losing the only home they had.

popularCulture.title: The Wretched of the Earth — Frantz Fanon (1961)
popularCulture.description: Fanon wrote this book during the war as a call for colonized people to throw off their rulers. It spread far beyond Algeria and shaped movements around the world. The book turns the Algerian fight into a wider argument about violence and freedom. In doing so it can blur the specific, often brutal facts of the actual war that inspired it.

popularCulture.title: Hors-la-loi (Outside the Law) — Rachid Bouchareb (2010)
popularCulture.description: This French-Algerian film follows three brothers drawn into the independence struggle, including its violence in France itself. It sparked protests in France when it came out. The film puts Algerian fighters at the center of their own story. Telling it through one family can smooth over how divided and ruthless the movement was toward its own people.

popularCulture.title: The harkis in French memory
popularCulture.description: The harkis were Algerians who fought for France and were then mostly left behind in 1962, when many were killed. In France their fate became a long, painful argument about debt and shame. The word now stands for people abandoned by the side they served. The simple image of loyal victims can hide how mixed and forced their wartime choices often were.
